interpretation: The Basic Law's inviolable human dignity provision (Art. 1 GG) and eternity clause (Art. 79(3) GG) are direct constitutional embodiments of high corrigibility: the postwar symbolic order structurally limits its own capacity for pathological escalation by placing individual dignity beyond collective override.

confidence.label: moderate-high
confidence.rationale: Interpretation is well-grounded in the constitutional text and contrasts cleanly with the Nazi Germany sacrificial-escalation case. Chain is currently a minimal stub.

mechanismSummary: The founding constitutional document embeds human dignity as inviolable and unamendable, directly blocking the collective-body logic that characterized the preceding pathological order.

interpretation: Article 20's resistance clause (Art. 20(4) GG) is the constitutive memory-driven-restraint provision of the postwar German symbolic order: the framers converted the Weimar experience of legal revolution — Hitler's use of enabling legislation to abolish parliamentary democracy through formally legal means — into a positive constitutional right to resist abolition of the democratic order. Combined with Art. 79(3)'s protection of Art. 20 as an eternity clause, this creates a two-layer corrigibility architecture: Art. 20 defines the protected democratic-social order; Art. 79(3) makes it unamendable; Art. 20(4) arms citizens to defend it when all other remedies fail. The memory of the pathological order is thus encoded as a constitutional right of resistance against its repetition — the clearest example of memory-driven-restraint in the dataset.

confidence.label: high
confidence.rationale: Text verified against official translation; scholarly consensus on framers' intent is clear (Weimar-lesson design); the Art. 20(4) / Art. 79(3) architectural relationship is analytically tight. Confidence capped: Art. 20(4) was added in 1968, not 1949, and the right has never been formally invoked, raising questions about its operative force.

mechanismSummary: Art. 20(1)+(4) converts the Weimar trauma of legal revolution into a constitutional right of resistance, encoding the memory of pathological collapse as a positive corrective mechanism — the democratic order cannot be legally abolished, and citizens are constitutionally empowered to prevent its abolition by other means.
